text: Hello, I am sharing a TME dissection for a low rectal cancer in a BMI-thirty-seven patient who is under five feet tall who got total neoadjuvant therapy for a T-four and two distal rectal cancer. I had a near complete response, could not even find a lesion, but MRI suggested still a T two and N one. So she was presented a tumor board and recommended for surgery.
The surgeon applies an ICG tattoo at the distal black ink tattoo mark, located one centimeter distal to the tumor, near the anorectal ring.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection to isolate the superior hemorrhoidal vessel, with the ureter visible in the surgical field. The small bowel is kept out of the way using a sponge, requiring frequent repositioning. The superior hemorrhoidal vein is seen adjacent to the artery, just distal to the takeoff of the left colic artery.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the isolated superior hemorrhoidal vessel during the TME dissection. The surgical field shows challenging tissue planes due to prior neoadjuvant therapy.

The surgeon carefully sweeps the nerves posteriorly, maintaining a plane against the glistening yellow mesorectum. Circumferential dissection progresses down the right side, with plans to move anteriorly while avoiding the vagina. The proximal black tattoo is visible a couple of centimeters above the lesion.
The surgeon performs a left lateral dissection to access the left-sided total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, joining the previously dissected medial plane.

The surgeon continues the posterior dissection with clear visualization of the surgical plane, moving down the left-hand side and identifying the left ureter. The dissection progresses to join the previously dissected anterior plane, followed by careful anterior dissection while avoiding the vagina and maintaining focus on the anterior mesorectum. The crackly plane is visible, joining circumferentially around the previous dissection.

The surgeon navigates the dissection plane, using the nerves as a guide to avoid going too posterior towards the sacrum. They continue circumferential dissection, working in areas that provide easier access, which improves retraction and facilitates further dissection in other directions.

The surgeon uses arm number one to push up against the vagina, working within the defined plane. The search for ICG tattoos continues, although they are not yet visible, indicating the need to proceed distally. Despite the patient's high BMI, the wide pelvis allows for clear visualization of the crackly plane posteriorly. The left-hand side dissection is less advanced but becomes clearer after completing the posterior and anterior dissections.

The surgeon returns to the anterior aspect, revealing the distal black tattoo, which is located one centimeter distal to the tumor as noted in the original colonoscopy. The ICG tattoo distal to the black tattoo is expected to come into view shortly. The surgeon maintains dissection within the RV septum.

The surgeon continues dissection downwards, aiming to keep the yellow fat attached to the rectum. Blunt dissection is utilized to reach below the distal extent of the anterior mesorectum, approaching the area where the rectum is adjacent to the vagina.

The surgeon continues to search for the ICG tattoo while navigating through the area with visible radiation fibrosis, presumed to be from tumor regression. The dissection is below the distal extent of the posterior mesorectum, with the levator muscles visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon continues circumferential dissection around the mesorectum, ensuring not to cone in on it, while maintaining exposure. A small tattoo becomes visible on the patient's right side. As the dissection progresses lower, the surgeon switches to a vessel sealer for improved control and hemostasis, addressing small blood vessels in the area.

The surgeon identifies the tattoo marking below the mesorectum and continues to free the mesorectum circumferentially. The tissue is being carefully dissected off the levators, and the surgeon switches back to scissors to reach further distal areas.

The surgeon dissects onto the peritoneal body, completing the total mesorectal excision (TME). The right-sided tattoo is visible, with the mesorectum removed, exposing the bare rectal wall. The surgeon prepares to staple beneath this area.

The surgeon ensures the mesorectum is circumferentially free to avoid entrapment in the stapler. The camera is repositioned to port two, and the stapler is placed in port three for a vertical anterior to posterior stapling approach. The surgeon checks the posterior view, identifying the unicoxygel ligament leading into the levators, and confirms the stapling position below the tattoo before firing.

The surgeon completes the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, examining the pelvic area to assess the final outcome. The coloanal anastomosis is visible, along with the diverting loop ileostomy.
